name: Quasar
explanation: An organismic patchable mono synth-voice for the experimentally minded - a wild instrument that pushes back. Two oscillators cross-modulate with through-zero FM and a feedback path, so small patch moves bloom into clangy, unpredictable, living textures. A resonant ladder low-pass, snappy ADSR, free LFO, sample & hold and noise round it out. Fully patchable: a 5x4 modulation matrix (LFO, Env, S&H, Noise, Osc2 into Pitch, Cutoff, PWM and Chaos) whose routing amounts are real parameters - cabling a source to a destination turns that amount up, so the patch you see is the patch you hear. Normalled Env-to-Cutoff and a touch of LFO-to-PWM. Not for straight subtractive sounds - for instruments that surprise you.

name: Dynasty
explanation: A lush, four-oscillator, paraphonic-voiced semi-modular mono synth with a proper stereo analog-style ping-pong delay - the flagship of the range. Four detuned oscillators stack into a huge unison, run through a warm 4-pole ladder low-pass with a snappy ADSR, then a wide stereo delay for size and motion. A free LFO, sample & hold and noise round it out. Fully patchable: a 5x4 modulation matrix (LFO, Env, S&H, Noise, Osc2 into Pitch, Cutoff, PWM and Delay) whose routing amounts are real parameters - cabling a source to a destination turns that amount up, so the patch you see is the patch you hear. Normalled Env-to-Cutoff and a touch of LFO-to-PWM. Lush and huge-sounding.

name: Boreal
explanation: A big, aggressive three-oscillator semi-modular mono synth for people who find typical semi-modulars too tame. Three detuned oscillators - a fat saw stack with a sub - drive a snarling resonant ladder low-pass with hard drive and a built-in spring reverb for size. A snappy ADSR, free LFO, sample & hold and noise round it out. Fully patchable: a 5x4 modulation matrix (LFO, Env, S&H, Noise, Osc2 into Pitch, Cutoff, PWM and Verb) whose routing amounts are real parameters - cabling a source to a destination turns that amount up, so the patch you see is the patch you hear. Normalled Env-to-Cutoff and a touch of LFO-to-PWM. Mono, last-note priority.

name: Cascara
explanation: A three-oscillator semi-modular mono synth that blends east and west coast in one voice: three detuned oscillators, with a fifth-stacked third, sum into a gentle wavefolder then a warm 4-pole ladder low-pass, so you can dial from clean subtractive to metallic folded timbre. Praised for staying rock-solid in tune. A snappy ADSR, free LFO, sample & hold and noise round it out. Fully patchable: a 5x4 modulation matrix (LFO, Env, S&H, Noise, Osc2 into Pitch, Cutoff, PWM and Verb) whose routing amounts are real parameters - cabling a source to a destination turns that amount up, so the patch you see is the patch you hear. Normalled Env-to-Cutoff and a touch of LFO-to-PWM. Mono, last-note priority.

name: Twin Grit
explanation: A bright, gritty two-oscillator semi-modular mono synth in the Japanese dual-filter lineage. Two oscillators run through two aggressive, self-oscillating filters in series - a resonant low-pass then a resonant high-pass - for the unmistakable honking, screaming character. A snappy ADSR, free LFO, sample & hold and noise feed a 5x4 patch matrix (LFO, Env, S&H, Noise, Osc2 into Pitch, LP Cut, PWM and HP Cut) whose routing amounts are real parameters - cabling a source to a destination turns that amount up, so the patch you see is the patch you hear. Normalled Env-to-LP Cut and a touch of LFO-to-PWM. Bright, gritty, unmistakable. Mono, last-note priority.

name: Shoreless
explanation: A no-coast tabletop mono synth that borrows from both east and west without committing to either. A single sine/triangle core runs into a smooth wavefolder - the west-coast overtone move - then a gentle low-pass, so timbre grows by folding rather than subtraction, for a distinctive, more experimental voice. A slope-style ADSR, free LFO, a random source and noise feed a 5x4 patch matrix (LFO, Env, Rand, S&H, Osc2 into Pitch, Cutoff, Fold and Air) whose routing amounts are real parameters - cabling a source to a destination turns that amount up. Normalled Env-to-Cutoff and a touch of LFO-to-Fold. Small, deep, endlessly rewarding. Mono.

name: Nucleon
explanation: A budget-champion dual-VCO semi-modular mono synth with a fat, detuned oscillator core (a CEM-style saw partner), a resonant low-pass and a hot overdrive output stage for thick, snarling bass and leads. A snappy ADSR, free LFO, sample & hold and noise round it out. Fully patchable: a 5x4 modulation matrix (LFO, Env, S&H, Noise, Osc2 into Pitch, Cutoff, PWM and Overdrive) whose routing amounts are real parameters - cabling a source to a destination turns that amount up, so the patch you see is the patch you hear. Normalled Env-to-Cutoff and a touch of LFO-to-PWM. Mono, last-note priority.

name: Feral
explanation: A raw, aggressive two-oscillator semi-modular mono synth in the Steiner-Parker lineage. Two oscillators (saw-to-pulse morph, osc2 tunable across an interval) drive a snarling resonant low-pass, and the signature is Grind: a feedback circuit that folds the output back into the filter for dirt, honk and chaos. A snappy ADSR, free LFO, sample & hold and noise round it out. Fully patchable: a 5x4 modulation matrix (LFO, Env, S&H, Noise, Osc2 into Pitch, Cutoff, PWM and Grind) whose routing amounts are real parameters - cabling a source to a destination turns that amount up, so the patch you see is the patch you hear. Normalled Env-to-Cutoff and a touch of LFO-to-PWM. Mono, last-note priority.

name: Progeny
explanation: A single-oscillator semi-modular mono synth built around an 8-step analog-style sequencer - the tabletop that launched a modern semi-modular line. One saw/pulse oscillator with a variable pulse width plus a square sub runs into a warm 4-pole transistor-ladder low-pass with a snappy ADSR, a free LFO, sample & hold, noise and a clocked step sequencer that keeps a held note dancing. Its signature is the patch bay: a 5x4 modulation matrix (LFO, Env, Seq, S&H, Noise into Pitch, Cutoff, PWM and Verb) whose routing amounts are real parameters - cabling a source to a destination turns that amount up, so the patch you see is the patch you hear. Normalled Seq-to-Pitch and Env-to-Cutoff make it sequence out of the box. Mono, last-note priority.

name: Forebear
explanation: A friendly two-oscillator semi-modular mono synth in the American ladder-filter lineage. Two Minimoog-style oscillators (saw-to-square morph, osc2 tunable across an interval) sum into a warm 4-pole transistor-ladder low-pass with a snappy ADSR, a free-running LFO, a clocked sample & hold, noise and a built-in spring reverb. Its signature is the patchbay: a 5x4 modulation matrix (LFO, Env, S&H, Noise, Osc2 into Pitch, Cutoff, PWM and Reverb) whose routing amounts are real parameters - cabling a source to a destination turns that amount up, so the patch you see is the patch you hear. Normalled Env-to-Cutoff and a touch of LFO-to-PWM make it sing unpatched. Mono, last-note priority.

name: Scout Mono
explanation: A semi-modular monosynth with sample & hold in the Roland System-100 lineage. Two oscillators (saw plus square) run through a resonant low-pass with its own envelope, and the signature patch is a noise sample & hold: it steps a new random value at S&H Rate and holds it, modulating the filter cutoff (and a touch of pitch) by S&H Depth for the burbling, sci-fi 'random step' movement that keeps a held note alive. Mono, last-note priority. Controls: Cutoff, Resonance, S&H Rate, S&H Depth, Env Amount and Level.

name: Fold West
explanation: A West-Coast complex-oscillator wavefolder in the Serge modular lineage. Instead of subtracting harmonics with a filter, it adds them by folding: a sine/triangle core (morphed by Timbre) is driven into a smooth wavefolder whose depth blooms with an envelope, so a held note grows brighter and more metallic then settles - the classic Buchla/Serge 'timbre from folding' movement. A gentle low-pass tames the top. 6-voice poly. Controls: Fold (fold depth), Timbre (sine to triangle), Cutoff, Env (fold-bloom amount), Decay and Level.

name: Patch Grid
explanation: A patch-matrix modular voice pushed toward the EMS Synthi 100. Four oscillators (saw, sine, triangle, pulse), a coloured noise generator, a ring modulator (OSC1 x OSC2), a trapezoid envelope and a high-resonance, near self-oscillating state-variable filter feed a spring-style reverb and output. The heart is a real 7x7 pin matrix: every source (OSC1-4, NOISE, RING, ENV) can be patched into every destination (each oscillator's FM input, filter cutoff, filter audio input, and the output) with an independent amount the audio engine reads every sample - anything modulates or feeds anything, just like the Synthi. Nothing patched to FILT-IN or OUT means silence, exactly like a real pin board. Mono, last-note priority. Knobs: Osc2/3/4 tune, Noise colour, Cutoff, Resonance, Attack, Decay, Reverb, Level.

name: Rover Mono
explanation: A budget two-oscillator Moog monosynth in the Moog Rogue lineage. Two sawtooth oscillators: osc1 tracks the note while Osc2 Interval tunes the second up to plus or minus an octave for fat detune, unison, fifths or octave stacks, and Osc Mix balances the pair. They run through a warm, resonant four-pole Moog-style ladder low-pass with its own envelope for the classic squelchy Moog bass and lead. Mono, last-note priority. Controls: Cutoff, Resonance, Env Amount, Osc2 Interval, Osc Mix and Level.

name: Realm FM
explanation: An FM-plus-filter hybrid synth in the Yamaha SY77 / SY99 'RCM' lineage. Where a pure FM synth is all operators, Realm FM runs a two-operator FM carrier through a resonant analog-style low-pass with its own envelope, pairing digital FM with subtractive shaping. Ratio and Index set the FM timbre; Cutoff and Env Amount sweep the filter for the evolving, vocal RCM character; Decay sets the amp contour. 8-voice poly. Controls: Ratio, Index, Cutoff, Env Amount, Decay and Level.

name: Polar Six
explanation: A warm 6-voice analog polysynth in the Sequential / Chroma Polaris lineage. Its signature is oscillator hard sync: a second sawtooth is locked to the first's period but runs at a higher ratio set by Sync, so sweeping Sync gives the classic tearing sync-lead harmonics. A third gently-detuned saw layer (Detune) adds analog warmth, and a smooth resonant two-pole low-pass with its own envelope shapes the tone. 6-voice poly. Controls: Cutoff, Resonance, Env Amount, Sync (sync ratio), Detune (warmth layer) and Level.

name: Swing Kit
explanation: A punchy sampled-style drum kit with SWING in the Akai MPC60 / 3000 lineage. The MPC's two claims to fame are its punchy, slightly saturated drum sound and its legendary timing swing. Swing Kit is a preset 16-step boom-bap groove of six punchy synthesised voices (kick, snare, closed and open hats, clap, perc); the signature Swing control drags every off-beat 16th late for that head-nodding MPC feel, while Punch boosts the transients and adds a touch of drive. Holding a note runs the groove; release stops it. Controls: Tune, Decay, Snap, Swing, Punch and Level.

name: Halo Ensemble
explanation: A lush preset ensemble polysynth in the Korg Lambda / Sigma lineage - a fully-polyphonic string/organ machine. Each of 8 voices runs two detuned oscillators morphed by Tone from bowed strings (saw) toward hollow organ (pulse) through a low-pass, and the whole poly mix passes into the signature Ensemble: a three-tap BBD-style modulated chorus that gives the wide, shimmering string-machine wash. Shimmer widens the per-voice detune. Controls: Tone, Cutoff, Attack, Ensemble (chorus depth), Shimmer (detune) and Level.

name: Rack Cell
explanation: A crisp 12-bit rack sampler in the Akai S900 / S1000 lineage. It plays a baked, self-authored CC0 sample - a bright mallet/marimba tone with a prominent tuned overtone and a sharp attack click, kept clean at 12-bit for the crisp Akai clarity - pitched across the keyboard with an amp envelope and a resonant low-pass. The Crunch control can re-crush the playback down to lo-fi grit when wanted. 6-voice poly. Controls: Tune, Cutoff, Decay, Crunch, Attack and Level.

name: Silk Cell
explanation: A warm 8-bit sampler in the E-mu Emulator II lineage. It plays a baked, self-authored CC0 sample - a warm detuned-saw string/choir ensemble tone, gently low-passed and 8-bit crushed for the lush Emulator II warmth - pitched across the keyboard with an amp envelope and a resonant low-pass. A gentle Vibrato LFO keeps a held note alive. 6-voice poly. Controls: Tune, Cutoff, Attack, Decay, Vibrato and Level.

name: Vox Shifter
explanation: A formant-aware vocal pitch shifter in the PSOLA / phase-vocoder family. A twin-grain crossfaded delay-line pitch shifter moves the pitch (Shift, plus/minus 12 semitones) click-free, while an independent Formant control sweeps a pair of resonant vowel formants over the shifted voice, so you can pitch a voice up without the chipmunk formant collapse, or slide the formants for gender and vowel morphs. Mix blends dry and wet; Output trims level. Controls: Shift, Formant, Mix, Output.

name: Fair Cell
explanation: An 8-bit sampler in the Fairlight CMI lineage. It plays a baked, self-authored CC0 sample - a morphing formant-vocal 'aah-to-ooh' tone, bit-crushed to 8-bit for the gritty digital Fairlight character - pitched across the keyboard with an amp envelope and a resonant low-pass. The signature Crunch re-crushes the playback for even more of that early-digital-sampler grain. 6-voice poly. Controls: Tune, Cutoff, Decay, Crunch, Attack and Level.

name: Blue Flanger
explanation: A deep, resonant, wide-stereo flanger in the Boss BF-2 lineage. Distinct from the factory's other flangers by its lush, watery voice: a long swept fractional delay (~0.3-12 ms) with a strong resonant positive Feedback that can ring into metallic territory, and a stereo Width control that offsets the LFO between the left and right channels for a wide underwater spread. A triangle LFO sweeps the delay around a Manual base time. Controls: Rate, Depth, Feedback, Manual, Width and Mix.

name: Grit Wave
explanation: A gritty digital wavetable polysynth in the Waldorf Microwave lineage. Sister to the smooth Wave Storm but harsher and more aliased: an 8-frame wavetable (built at init, from near-sine up to bright formant-heavy frames) is scanned by Position; the signature Scan sweeps that position with an LFO so the timbre morphs on a single held note; and Drive adds saturation plus digital grain for the characterful edge. 8-voice poly through a resonant two-pole low-pass. Controls: Cutoff, Resonance, Position (wavetable frame), Scan (LFO sweep speed and depth), Drive (grit) and Level.

name: Thin Kit
explanation: A cheap, thin, dry 12-bit sampling drum box in the Casio RZ-1 lineage. Where the factory's other boxes are warm, crunchy or glassy, this one is small and papery: a short thin kick, a papery snare, tight hats, a clicky rimshot, a small cowbell and a dry clap. A mild 12-bit crush plus a global BRIGHT high-pass give the cheap-PCM character. Like the original it is a preset PATTERN box: hold a note to start an internal 16-step pop groove, release to stop and let the tails ring. Controls: Tune (kick pitch), Decay, Snap (snare noise vs body), Bright (global high-pass / thinness), Accent and Level.

name: Pulse Kit
explanation: A bright digital/FM drum machine in the Yamaha RX5 lineage. Where the warm boxes are round and the 8-bit box is crunchy, this one is glassy and metallic: FM kick, FM snare, inharmonic FM toms, glassy FM hats and a dense shimmering FM crash, all synthesised in real time with two-operator FM for the bright 80s digital-percussion character. Like the original it is a preset PATTERN box: hold a note to start an internal 16-step groove with a tom fill, release to stop and let the tails ring. Controls: Tune (kick/tom pitch), Decay, Snap (snare noise vs tone), Metal (FM brightness of hats/toms/crash), Accent and Level.

name: FM Core
explanation: A clean two-operator FM synth built on the textbook Chowning algorithm: one modulator phase-modulates one carrier. Ratio sets the modulator:carrier frequency relationship (quantised to musical half-integers), Index sets the FM depth, and the signature Index Env makes that depth decay over each note so the tone starts bright and bell-like then settles. Operator Feedback adds extra harmonics and edge. 8-voice poly. Controls: Ratio, Index, Index Env, Feedback, Decay and Level.

name: Vector Eight
explanation: A vector-synthesis polysynth in the Sequential Prophet VS lineage. Four oscillators (sine, saw, square and a hollow-digital tone) sit at the corners of a vector square and are blended by an X/Y position. The signature SCAN orbits that position with an LFO so the timbre is in constant motion even on a single held chord. 8-voice poly through a resonant two-pole low-pass. Controls: Cutoff, Resonance, Vector X, Vector Y, Scan (orbit speed and depth) and Level.

name: Byte Beat
explanation: A gritty 8-bit lo-fi drum machine in the E-mu Drumulator lineage: the crunchy, dusty early-80s sampling drum sound beloved of hip-hop. Six synthesised voices (kick, snare, hat, clap, cowbell, tom) run through a global BIT-CRUSH plus sample-rate reducer that gives the signature 8-bit grit. Like the original it is a preset PATTERN box: hold a note to start an internal 16-step boom-bap groove, release to stop and let the tails ring. Controls: Tune (drum pitch), Decay, Crunch (bit-depth and downsample grit), Hat (brightness), Accent and Level.

name: Prism Eight
explanation: A flexible, slightly metallic 8-voice analog/hybrid polysynth in the Rhodes Chroma lineage. Its signature is RING modulation between the two oscillators: turn Ring up and the detuned saws cross into clangorous, bell-like, inharmonic territory the way the Chroma's patchable voice could. Each voice runs two detuned saws through a resonant two-pole low-pass with its own envelope. Controls: Cutoff, Resonance, Env Amount, Ring (ring-mod depth), Detune and Level.

name: Crisp Beat
explanation: A crisp, dry, punchy digital drum machine in the Roland TR-707 / 727 lineage and the clean opposite of the warm Velvet Rhythm: a tight clicky kick, a snappy bright snare, crisp closed and open hats, a layered hand-clap and a short cowbell, all synthesised in real time. Like the original it is a preset PATTERN box: hold a note to start an internal 16-step four-on-the-floor groove, release to stop and let the tails ring. Controls: Tune (kick pitch), Decay, Snap (snare noise vs tone), Hat (hat brightness and length), Accent and Level.

name: Onyx Eight
explanation: A bright, punchy 8-voice analog polysynth in the Oberheim OB-8 lineage. Sister to the warm 2-pole Voice Eight but steeper and brighter: each voice runs a sawtooth plus a pulse whose width is swept by a per-patch PWM LFO, through a cascaded FOUR-POLE resonant low-pass with its own envelope, and a unison Spread detunes the oscillators for the classic brassy OB pad. Controls: Cutoff, Resonance, Env Amount, PWM (pulse-width sweep depth), Spread (unison detune) and Level.

name: Velvet Rhythm
explanation: A warm boutique preset-rhythm box in the Roland CR-78 CompuRhythm lineage. Unlike a pad-per-drum kit, this is a preset PATTERN player: hold a note and it starts an internal 16-step groove layering a warm rounded bass drum, a soft tonal snare, a sizzly metallic hi-hat, the signature inharmonic twin-square metallic beat, a woody conga and a papery maraca, all synthesised in real time; release to stop and let the tails ring. Controls: Tone (drum pitch), Decay, Snap (snare noise vs body), Metal (metallic-beat / hat brightness and ring), Accent and Level.

name: Multi Mono
explanation: An expressive single-VCO monosynth in the Multimoog lineage. One oscillator (sawtooth plus variable pulse) and a square sub run through a Moog-style resonant low-pass with its own envelope; the signature OSC MOD applies an LFO to both pitch (vibrato) and pulse-width for the lip/aftertouch-style growl and expression. Last-note mono with glide. Controls: Cutoff, Resonance, Env Amount, Osc Mod, Sub and Level.

name: Voice Eight
explanation: A fat unison analog polysynth in the Oberheim Four/Eight Voice lineage. Each voice stacks two detuned sawtooths plus a square sub through a smooth resonant low-pass with its own envelope, with a wide unison Spread for the huge stacked-SEM choir/brass. 8-voice poly. Controls: Cutoff, Resonance, Env Amount, Spread (unison detune), Sub and Level.

name: Pitch Snap
explanation: A real-time pitch corrector in the Antares Auto-Tune lineage. It detects the input's pitch by autocorrelation, snaps it to the nearest note of the chosen scale, and shifts the audio to that pitch with a crossfaded two-grain delay-line shifter. Fast retune gives the robotic 'T-Pain' snap; slow gives natural correction. Controls: Speed (retune time), Amount (correction strength), Key (chromatic / major / minor / fifths), Mix and Output. Works best on monophonic, pitched input.

name: Wave Storm
explanation: A wavetable-scanning synth in the Waldorf Blofeld lineage — distinct from the factory's fixed-wave PPG/Wavestation units: a smooth wavetable of eight single-cycle frames (built at init from harmonic spectra) is scanned continuously by a Position control and swept by the envelope (Scan), so the timbre morphs over each note. Resonant low-pass with its own envelope, amp envelope, 8-voice poly. Controls: Position, Scan (envelope→position), Cutoff, Resonance, Env Amount and Level.

name: Analog Bucket
explanation: A generic bucket-brigade (BBD) model: a short, dark, companded delay that doubles as a chorus/vibrato. A modulated BBD delay line with progressively darker repeats and gentle compander grit — at short times with high Mod it's a lush chorus, at longer times a warm analog echo. Controls: Time, Feedback, Mod (chorus depth), Tone (BBD darkness) and Mix.

name: Soft Opto
explanation: A smooth optical leveling amplifier (generic opto-compressor model). A slow photocell-style detector gives gentle, program-dependent gain reduction with a soft knee and a touch of warmth — gentler and slower than the factory's other compressors. Controls: Threshold, Ratio, Makeup, Tone (post tilt) and Mix.

name: Source Mono
explanation: A smooth two-oscillator monosynth in the Moog Source lineage. Two oscillators (saw + pulse, blendable) plus a square sub feed a resonant ladder-style low-pass with its own envelope, with glide and an amp envelope. Warm, rounded, preset-classic Moog mono. Controls: Cutoff, Resonance, Env Amount, Osc Mix (saw↔pulse), Detune (osc-2) and Level.

name: Micro Beat
explanation: A tiny budget analog drum box in the Boss DR-110 lineage — distinct from the factory's bigger drum machines by a thin, clicky, bright character: a short clicky kick, a papery snare, sizzly 'tssh' hats and a thin clap/cymbal, all synthesised in real time. A MIDI note selects a voice (note % 5) into a 6-slot pool. Controls: Pitch, Decay, Click (attack snap), Hat (brightness/length), Accent and Level.

name: Hex Glow
explanation: A 6-voice single-VCO polysynth in the Korg Polysix lineage. One VCO (saw) plus a square sub per voice, a resonant low-pass with its own envelope and an amp envelope; the whole poly mix runs through the Polysix's signature effects section — here a 4-stage modulated allpass phaser (the distinct hook versus the factory's chorus polys). Controls: Cutoff, Resonance, Env Amount, Sub, Phaser and Level.

name: Compander
explanation: A companding 'noise-reduction character' processor in the Dolby A/SR + HX lineage. Distinct from the factory's studio compressors: a level-dependent compress→expand with high-frequency pre-emphasis that imparts the characteristic tape-NR sheen and gentle breathing — used as a tone/dynamics colour. Controls: Squeeze (compand depth), HF (high-shelf emphasis), Tone (tilt), Output and Mix.

name: Oracle Six
explanation: A CEM-based analog polysynth in the Sequential Prophet-600 lineage. Two oscillators (saw + pulse) per voice; the signature Poly-Mod routes the filter envelope to oscillator pitch for sweeping, sync-like and clangourous timbres. Resonant low-pass with its own envelope, amp envelope, 6-voice poly. Controls: Cutoff, Resonance, Env Amount, Poly-Mod, Detune and Level.

name: Tri Poly
explanation: A bright DCO polysynth in the Roland JX-3P lineage. Two DCOs (saw + pulse) per voice with OSC-2 -> OSC-1 cross-modulation for a metallic edge, a resonant low-pass with its own decay envelope, and a built-in chorus for the lush JX shimmer. 8-voice poly. Controls: Cutoff, Resonance, Env Amount, Cross-Mod, Chorus and Level.

name: Pocket Rhythm
explanation: A vintage Latin preset rhythm box in the Korg Mini-Pops lineage — distinct from the factory's kick/snare drum machines: an analog percussion set synthesised in real time (bongo, conga, claves, maracas, cowbell and cymbal). A MIDI note picks a voice (note % 6) into an 8-slot pool. Controls: Pitch (membrane tuning), Decay, Shaker (maracas/cymbal length), Cowbell (level/tone), Tone and Level.

name: Aurora VA
explanation: A clean modern virtual-analog lead in the Nord Lead lineage. Distinct from a hypersaw VA: a single morphable oscillator whose Shape control sweeps continuously from sine through triangle and sawtooth to pulse, with oscillator Sync for bite, a clean resonant low-pass with its own envelope, and a snappy amp envelope. 8-voice poly. Controls: Shape (waveform morph), Cutoff, Resonance, Sync, Env Amount and Level.

name: Lately FM
explanation: A 2-operator FM voice with selectable operator waveforms, in the Yamaha TX81Z lineage. Distinct from sine-only FM synths: choosing non-sine operator waves (half-sine, abs-sine, quarter, even, square-ish) gives reedy, buzzy, hollow timbres — the famous 'Lately Bass'. A modulator with feedback phase-modulates a carrier, both using the selected wave, with a brightness (modulator) decay and ADSR. 8-voice poly. Controls: Wave (operator waveform), Ratio, FM Depth, Feedback, Decay and Level.

name: Bark Beat
explanation: A tight analog drum machine in the Roland TR-606 lineage — every hit is synthesised in real time (no samples). Distinct from the 808/909 and Simmons units: a punchy 'barking' kick with a fast pitch-drop, a snappy noise+tone snare, tight analog toms and bright sizzly high-passed hats. A MIDI note selects a voice (note % 6) into an 8-slot pool. Controls: Pitch, Punch (kick attack/snap), Snare (tone/noise), Hat (brightness + length), Decay and Level.

name: Vocal Plate
explanation: A bright, dense studio plate reverb in the EMT 140 lineage — the classic vocal and snare plate, brighter and longer than an algorithmic Dattorro plate. An input diffusion chain (four series allpasses) feeds a lightly-modulated figure-of-eight tank (two allpasses and two long delays with high-frequency damping) for a lush, shimmering metallic-to-smooth tail. Controls: dry/wet Mix, Decay, Tone (brightness), Pre-Delay and plate Size.

name: Divide Ensemble
explanation: A divide-down string + organ machine in the Roland RS-09 / Logan String Melody lineage. Distinct from lush Solina/Crumar string ensembles: it blends a Strings voice (sawtooth) and an Organ voice (divide-down square) per key, with slow attack/release and a wide three-voice ensemble chorus. Fully polyphonic (12 voices). Controls: Strings, Organ, Attack, Release, Ensemble and Level.

name: Vee Organ
explanation: A bright 'combo' organ in the Vox Continental lineage — hollower and brighter than a reedy Farfisa. Each key sums drawbar-weighted harmonic partials with a key-click transient, a pitch vibrato and a brightness/tone control; fully polyphonic (12 voices). Controls: Low (16'+8' drawbars), High (4'+2'+upper drawbars), Brightness, Vibrato, Click and Level.

name: Hex Drums
explanation: Analog electronic drums in the Simmons SDS-V lineage — every hit is synthesised in real time, not sampled. The signature is the pitch-swept 'pew' tom: a sine whose pitch drops sharply at the attack, plus a noisy click, with kick, snare and hat voices. A MIDI note selects a voice (note % 6) and fires it into an 8-slot pool. Controls: Pitch (tuning), Sweep (the pitch-drop depth), Decay, Noise (click/snap), Tone and Level.

name: Brass Eight
explanation: A fat 8-voice analog polysynth in the Oberheim OB-Xa lineage — brighter and harder than an OB-X, built for big brass stabs and bright pads. Two detuned oscillators (saw + pulse) per voice give a thick unison; a resonant state-variable filter whose Mode control morphs low-pass -> band-pass -> high-pass shapes the tone, with its own filter envelope. Controls: Cutoff, Resonance, Mode (LP/BP/HP morph), Env Amount, Detune (unison fatness) and Level.

name: Nova Six
explanation: A 6-voice DCO polysynth in the Juno-106 lineage. A single stable DCO (saw + variable pulse with PWM) plus a square sub feeds a resonant low-pass with its own decay envelope, then a non-resonant high-pass that carves the low end (its signature move), into a lush BBD-style chorus. Controls: Cutoff, Resonance, HPF (high-pass), Env Amount, Chorus and Level.

name: Linear Dream
explanation: An LA-synthesis voice in the late-80s digital lineage. Each note layers a short PCM attack transient (selectable: mallet, blown or pluck) over a synthesised sustain body — a sawtooth through a per-voice resonant low-pass with a decaying filter envelope — then through a lush built-in chorus. 8-voice polyphonic. Controls: Attack (transient type), Cutoff, Resonance, Env Amount (filter envelope), Chorus and Level. The attack samples are original, self-authored CC0 content synthesised offline (no third-party samples).

name: Dust Keys
explanation: A lo-fi 8-bit melodic sampler. One original sampled instrument voice (a warm detuned choir/string pad) is baked in as PCM and played back polyphonically, pitch-tracked with a sustain loop. The signature grit comes from a lo-fi stage — bit-depth reduction plus sample-rate decimation (the Bits knob) — feeding a resonant low-pass filter and an ADSR. Controls: Cutoff, Resonance, Bits (lo-fi crush), Attack, Decay, Level. The sampled voice is original, self-authored CC0 content synthesised offline (no third-party samples).

name: Crystal Station
explanation: A PCM-workstation synth in the late-80s digital lineage. Three original sampled voices — a bright percussive Piano, an airy Universe pad, and an FM Bell — are baked in as PCM and played back polyphonically, pitch-tracked across the keyboard, with an attack + decay amp envelope, a tone filter and a lush built-in stereo chorus. Controls: Voice (Piano/Universe/Bell), Attack, Decay, Tone, Chorus and Level. The PCM voices are original, self-authored CC0 content synthesised offline (no third-party samples).

name: Kit Machine
explanation: A sample-playback drum machine. Eight short drum one-shots (kick, snare, closed and open hat, clap, tom, rim, cowbell) are baked into the plugin as PCM and played back polyphonically — a true sampler engine, distinct from real-time-synthesised analog drum boxes. A MIDI note selects a voice (note modulo 8) and fires a one-shot; global Tune, Decay, Snap (attack), Tone and Level shape the whole kit. The drum samples are original, self-authored CC0 content synthesised offline (no third-party samples).

name: Edge Echo
explanation: A bright rackmount digital delay with a coloured input preamp. The preamp adds drive and presence for the punchy, present 'edge' tone, feeding clean, slightly bright digital repeats with filtered feedback. Controls: preamp Drive, delay Time (20 ms to ~1.2 s), Feedback, repeat Tone and dry/wet Mix.

name: Mini Volt
explanation: A compact, expressive single-oscillator mono synth: one wide-range main oscillator (saw blended with a PWM pulse) plus a square sub an octave down, feeding a 4-pole resonant ladder low-pass with its own snappy filter envelope. Osc Mod adds a small LFO that vibratos the pitch and breathes the pulse width for vocal growl, Glide slides pitch between notes (last-note priority), and Sub thickens the low end. Cutoff and Resonance sing; the whole voice stays mono and bounded.

name: Seq Bass
explanation: A squelchy monophonic acid sequencer-bass synth: one VCO mixing a saw+square core with a square sub-oscillator an octave down for a fuller voice, feeding the famous squelchy 4-pole resonant low-pass driven by a snappy decay envelope. Accent emphasises the filter sweep and level for punchy acid hits, Glide slides the pitch between notes for sliding lines, and high Resonance squelches toward self-oscillation while staying bounded. Monophonic, last-note priority, pure algorithm.

name: Flutter Echo
explanation: A physically-modelled tape delay that foregrounds the wow and flutter of an unstable tape transport. The read head is modulated by a slow wow LFO plus a faster flutter LFO with a little drift, so every repeat is audibly pitch-warbled; each pass through the loop adds tape saturation and high-frequency loss, so echoes darken and degrade as they stack. The Warble control scales the modulation from subtle vintage warmth to seasick wobble. Controls: Time, Feedback, Warble, Tone and dry/wet Mix.

name: Rack Echo
explanation: A pristine 80s studio rack digital delay with a doubler mode. Clean, hi-fi repeats (no tape grit, no companding) run through a low-pass filtered feedback path so successive echoes gently roll off their highs. A subtle stereo LFO modulates the read position so a single repeat can be fattened into a wide chorused double or slapback. Controls: Time, Feedback, Modulation (doubler/chorus depth), Tone (repeat HF) and dry/wet Mix; Mix at 0 is dry.

name: Lab Mono
explanation: An aggressive, patchable single-VCO monophonic synth: one oscillator (saw + PWM pulse) drives a screaming Korg-35-style 2-pole resonant low-pass that sings toward self-oscillation as Resonance is pushed. A punchy decay envelope sweeps the cutoff, and a patchable MOD source — stepped sample & hold at slow rates, a smooth triangle LFO at fast rates — wires straight into the filter for burbling, zapping movement. Last-note-priority mono; pitch tracks each played note in Hz. Raw, experimental lab-synth grit, fully bounded.

name: Bolt Mono
explanation: A lean, punchy two-oscillator hard-sync mono synth in the compact American two-VCO lead/bass tradition. OSC-1 is a saw master; OSC-2 is a detuned pulse that hard-syncs to the master, resetting its phase every master cycle for the signature zappy, metallic sync edge. Both feed a punchy Moog-style 4-pole resonant ladder low-pass driven by a fast decay envelope, with quick glide and a snappy amp envelope. Sync adds the aggressive edge, Cutoff and Resonance snap, Detune fattens slightly, and the whole voice stays tight, fast and immediate — a small, aggressive lead/bass machine. Pure algorithm, no samples; pitch tracks each played note in Hz.

name: Stage Strings
explanation: A dual string + brass ensemble machine in the lineage of the 70s divide-down stage keyboards. Fully polyphonic: every held note drives two blendable sections that share its pitch — a bright, fast STRINGS section (three detuned saws plus an octave shimmer) and a slower, swelling BRASS section (a reedy pulse + saw pushed through a per-voice low-pass that opens with its own bloom envelope, so notes lean in from dark to bright). The two are mixed by the Strings / Brass levels, shaped by a shared Attack/Release contour, tilted by Tone, then run through a triple-tap ensemble chorus for the lush, wide 70s shimmer. Controls: Strings, Brass, Attack, Release, Ensemble, Tone and Level.

name: Glass Chorus
explanation: A pristine hi-fi stereo chorus/flanger combo. Two fractional delay lines (one per channel) are read with linear interpolation and modulated by quadrature LFOs so the left and right voices sweep in counter-phase for a wide, glassy stereo image. A single Mode control morphs the effect from a lush, gently detuned multi-voice chorus into a short, sweeping flanger comb; the Feedback control adds the resonant flange whoosh, Width spreads the stereo voices and Depth/Rate shape the sweep. The signal path stays clean with no saturation or grit. Controls: Rate, Depth, Mode, Feedback, Width and dry/wet Mix.

name: Twin Volt
explanation: A fat dual-VCO monophonic synth voice in the classic late-70s mono lineage. Two detuned analog-style oscillators (a saw and a pulse) plus a square sub-oscillator an octave down are summed and ALSO ring-modulated against each other, so the Ring control blends clangy, inharmonic metallic colour into the tone. The mix drives a punchy 4-pole resonant low-pass with its own decay envelope, then an amp envelope; pitch glides between notes. Great for fat basses and clangy leads. Controls: Cutoff, Resonance, Env Amount, Ring, Detune, Decay and Level.

name: Patch Tower
explanation: A colossal three-oscillator semi-modular monophonic synthesizer in the great modular-tower lineage. Three big detuned oscillators (band-limited saw, pulse and triangle) blend thick, joined by a dedicated white-noise channel and hard oscillator SYNC for metallic sweeps. Everything pours into a fat 4-pole transistor-ladder low-pass with tanh saturation in its feedback loop and its own sweeping filter envelope, with last-note-priority glide. Controls: Cutoff, Resonance, Env Amount, Detune (3-osc spread), Sync, Noise, Glide and master Level. Regal, towering and modular.

name: Clip Stage
explanation: A clean, transparent soft-clip saturator and drive stage — built for warmth, glue and gentle level control rather than grind. A makeup-compensated Drive feeds a selectable transfer curve: DIODE gives a symmetric soft knee with odd harmonics, while TUBE adds a small bias for asymmetric, even-harmonic warmth (an internal DC blocker removes the resulting offset). A subtle Tone tilt opens or darkens the top, an output Trim sets level, and a parallel Mix blends the saturated signal back against the dry. Controls: Drive, Curve (diode/tube), Tone, Trim and Mix.

name: Atrium
explanation: A natural, bright digital ambience room reverb. Cascaded input-diffusion allpasses scatter the signal into a dense early-reflection cloud that feeds an 8-line feedback delay network with a lossless Hadamard mix, giving a smooth, colourless, metallic-ring-free tail. A gentle high-frequency air shelf keeps the top open and the room sounds invisible rather than boxy. Controls: Mix, Size, Decay, Diffusion, Air and stereo Width.

name: Velour Poly
explanation: A warm, velvety velocity-sensitive DCO polyphonic synthesizer with up to eight voices. Each voice runs two digitally-clocked oscillators — a band-limited saw and a band-limited pulse — with gentle cross-modulation for a slightly hollow, glassy character, into a smooth resonant low-pass driven by its own envelope (Cutoff + Env Amount); an amplitude envelope and brightness track key velocity, and a stereo chorus widens it into a lush 80s pad. Controls: Cutoff, Resonance, Env Amount, Cross-Mod, Chorus, Attack, Release and Level.

name: Voyage Mono
explanation: A modern, hi-fi monophonic synthesizer in the modern transistor-ladder mono lead/bass tradition. Three fat oscillators (each a continuously variable saw-to-pulse blend) are spread by a Detune control for thick, beating unison, then run into a 4-pole transistor-ladder filter wrapped in a dual-mode blend: the Filter Mode knob morphs the response from a warm low-pass, through a spaced band-pass-like middle, toward an airy high-pass. A snappy per-note filter and amplitude envelope with bipolar Env Amount, portamento Glide and a clean soft-limited output stage complete the voice. Controls: Cutoff, Resonance, Filter Mode, Env Amount, Detune, Glide, Level.

name: Valve Rotary
explanation: A full organ rotary-speaker chain fed by an overdriven tube preamp. A warm valve stage with grid bias adds second-harmonic warmth that grows into a growl as Drive rises, then feeds a twin-rotor rotating speaker: the signal is split into a bass-rotor and a treble horn band, each spun through its own virtual driver for amplitude tremolo plus a short Doppler pitch vibrato. The horn turns faster than the drum in opposite phase, and Speed morphs slow chorale to fast tremolo with inertia so the rotors ramp rather than jump. Controls: Speed, Drive, Depth, Tone and dry/wet Mix.

name: Cozy Delay
explanation: A warm, dark, short bucket-brigade-style analog delay. A compact interpolated delay line (~20-300 ms) feeds a bounded feedback path; each repeat is made progressively darker and softer by a cascaded low-pass inside the loop plus gentle per-pass saturation, so a string of echoes melts from a present slapback into a soft, intimate shadow. A faint wow drift adds vintage warmth. Controls: Time, Feedback, Tone and dry/wet Mix.

name: Digi Wave
explanation: A hybrid digital/analog polyphonic synthesizer with up to eight voices. Each voice plays a selectable single-cycle digital waveform from a bank of eight harmonically-distinct tables (organ, reed, hollow square, piano-ish, buzzy saw, pulse, bell and sync-buzz) built at startup, then runs it through a warm resonant analog-style 4-pole low-pass with its own attack/release contour. A built-in stereo delay/chorus adds the signature shimmer. Controls: Wave select, Cutoff, Resonance, Env Amount, Attack, Release, Delay mix and Level. Digital edge meets analog warmth.

name: Warm Flanger
explanation: A lush, warm analog-voiced flanger in the bucket-brigade tradition. A short fractional delay line is swept by a slow triangle LFO around a parked centre set by Manual; Width sets how far the comb sweeps, Rate the speed, and Regen the feedback. The feedback path is gently low-passed and softly saturated so heavy Regen thickens and resonates creamily instead of turning clangy. A small stereo LFO offset gives a gentle moving image. Controls: Manual, Width, Rate, Regen and dry/wet Mix.

name: Quad Dimension
explanation: A clean, hi-fi dimensional stereo chorus with four fixed mode buttons instead of rate and depth knobs. Each mode (1 = subtle to 4 = widest) preselects a dimensional size that builds an anti-phase side signal from a pair of short, gently de-tuned delay taps per channel, widening and thickening the stereo image with almost no audible pitch wobble. The modulation is kept very slow and shallow so it shimmers rather than warbles; the mono sum stays stable. Controls: Mode (1..4), Width (stereo spread), Tone (dark to bright sparkle) and dry/wet Mix.

name: Master Glue
explanation: A classic British console-style mix-bus 'glue' compressor for gelling a whole mix into one cohesive, punchy whole. A feed-forward, stereo-LINKED VCA detector watches the summed program and drives a single soft-knee gain-reduction element computed in the dB domain, so the same reduction hits both channels and the stereo image stays rock-solid. Ratio is stepped between the classic 2:1, 4:1 and 10:1 settings, Attack is stepped, and Release offers four fixed times plus an AUTO position whose program-dependent dual time-constant 'breathes' with the music — fast on transients, slow under sustained loudness. Makeup restores level, Mix blends parallel dry for New-York glue, and a soft output stage keeps the bus bounded. Controls: Threshold, Ratio, Attack, Release, Makeup and Mix.

name: Tower Spring
explanation: An original model of a tall studio spring-reverb tank: a long cascade of dispersive all-pass lines smears transients into a clean, bright 'boing/chirp' that feeds a long, lightly-modulated stereo feedback delay for a smooth, hi-fi tail up to several seconds. Decay lengthens the tail, Tone tilts dark to bright, Boing sets the dispersion/chirp amount, Width spreads the two decorrelated spring loops, and Mix blends dry/wet.

name: Comet Six
explanation: A six-voice analog-style polyphonic synthesizer in the lineage of the early-80s multimode flagships. Each voice runs two oscillators — a band-limited saw and pulse — with oscillator cross-modulation and a touch of hard-sync grit for metallic edge, feeding a resonant state-variable filter whose MODE continuously morphs between low-pass, band-pass and high-pass for hollow, bright and nasal poly tones a plain low-pass cannot reach. A dedicated filter envelope and an amplitude AR shape every voice, so chords ring independently. Controls: Cutoff, Resonance, Filter Mode, Env Amount, Cross-Mod, Attack, Release and Level.

name: Wide Ensemble
explanation: A lush bucket-brigade (BBD) stereo chorus ensemble with a vibrato mode. A mono-summed input is fanned into a wide stereo image by two modulated delay lines whose sweep runs in anti-phase on the left and right, so the channels swirl against each other for a big ensemble. The Mode control morphs from chorus (dry plus modulated taps) to vibrato (pure modulated tap, so the whole signal pitch-wobbles). A BBD-darkness Tone low-passes the wet path and a soft saturation emulates compander grit for warm analog character. Controls: Rate, Depth, Mode, Tone and dry/wet Mix.

name: Amber Crunch
explanation: A compressed germanium hard-clip distortion in the two-knob vintage lineage. A single high-gain stage drives a pair of soft germanium/LED clipping diodes to ground, giving a smooth-yet-fuzzy, round and slightly dark distortion. Distortion sweeps from light grit to a thick compressed wall; a gentle post Tone tilt and an Output level finish it, with a dry/wet Mix. Controls: Distortion, Output, Tone and Mix.

name: Room Print
explanation: A convolution reverb that prints your sound into a real, measured acoustic space. A genuine room impulse response is baked into the plugin and convolved with the input in the time domain, so the reverb is the true fingerprint of a physical room rather than an algorithm. Controls: dry/wet Mix, Size (how much of the tail is used), Pre-Delay, Tone (high-frequency damping of the tail) and stereo Width. Impulse response: Adventure Kid 'AK-SROOMS' small-room IR, licensed CC BY 4.0.

name: Strip EQ
explanation: A four-band British console channel-strip equaliser. A low shelf and high shelf bookend two fully swept parametric mid bells (low-mid and high-mid, each with its own gain and frequency), and a sweepable high-pass filter cleans the bottom end before the EQ. Clean, surgical and punchy in the mixing-desk tradition. Controls: Low, Lo-Mid, Lo-Mid Freq, Hi-Mid, Hi-Mid Freq, High, HP and Output.

name: Funk Filter
explanation: An envelope-follower auto-wah: a resonant state-variable filter whose cutoff is swept by the input's own dynamics. Play louder and the peak swoops further; a peak-detecting envelope follower with snappy attack and a musical release tail drives the sweep. Direction flips it (up: louder opens the filter; down: louder closes it), Mode picks a low-pass or a vocal band-pass voice, Sensitivity sets how hard the envelope pushes, Resonance sets the Q/peak, and Range sets the span of the sweep. The classic funky, vocal 'wow' that tracks your playing. Controls: Sensitivity, Resonance, Range, Direction, Mode and dry/wet Mix.

name: Sub Station
explanation: A modern virtual-analog acid/electro bass mono synth: a saw oscillator and a hard-synced pulse oscillator (the Sync control sets OSC2's pitch above OSC1, tearing out a screaming sync edge) plus a sub one octave down for weight. The mix drives a snappy resonant low-pass that squelches and screams under its decay envelope, then a post Overdrive stage adds aggressive, slightly-digital grit for in-your-face acid lines and squelchy leads.

name: Sub Pedal
explanation: A dedicated deep sub-bass pedal synthesizer in the lineage of the classic wooden foot-pedal bass synths. HUGE and simple by design: two stacked main oscillators (saw + square) tuned low, plus a thick square SUB an octave below for chest-thumping weight, all driven through a fat 4-pole Moog-style transistor-ladder low-pass with tanh saturation in the feedback loop and a deliberately slow filter envelope. Monophonic with last-note-priority glide/portamento and a punchy amplitude envelope, finished by a warm overdrive stage that adds low-end weight and harmonics. Built for floor-rumbling root notes. Controls: Cutoff, Resonance, Sub, Glide, Decay, Drive and Level.

name: Arc Poly
explanation: An American-style polyphonic synth voice: a bank of up to 8 independent voices, each with its own VCO (saw + PWM pulse), its own 4-pole resonant low-pass with a decay envelope, and its own amp envelope — so holding a chord sounds every note at its own pitch. A shared SAMPLE & HOLD source samples a new random level at the S&H Rate and wobbles every voice's cutoff by S&H Depth in lock-step, giving the classic stepped-random ARP burble. Pitch tracks each played note in Hz.

name: Hold Echo
explanation: A studio digital delay with modulation and infinite hold. A clean delay line feeds linear-interpolated repeats whose read tap is gently swept by a quadrature LFO for chorus / pitch-wobble echoes; a tilt tone filter darkens or brightens the feedback path, and a Hold control lifts feedback toward unity while fading the input out so the captured buffer loops and freezes without runaway. A soft limiter keeps the loop bounded. Controls: Time, Feedback, Modulation, Tone, Hold and dry/wet Mix.

name: Steel Flanger
explanation: An extreme, metallic jet-engine flanger. A swept short delay with strong regeneration drives the comb filter into clangy, almost-pitched stainless resonance. A triangle LFO sweeps a fractional delay around a Manual base position over a very wide range, while bipolar Feedback intensifies the metallic ring (positive) or hollows it out (negative through-zero hiss). A soft saturator inside the feedback path keeps the resonance bounded but aggressive. Controls: Rate, Depth, Manual, Feedback and dry/wet Mix.

name: Sweet Drive
explanation: A warm asymmetric soft-clip overdrive. The signal is band-limited to clean the low end, then pushed into an asymmetric waveshaper that clips one half harder than the other — generating even harmonics for a sweeter, slightly fatter break-up over a gentle mid-hump. Touch-sensitive, amp-like dynamics; a post tone low-pass shapes brightness, with output Level and dry/wet Mix. Controls: Drive, Tone, Level, Mix.

name: Crush Comp
explanation: An aggressive FET-style compressor with built-in distortion. A fast, stereo-linked peak detector drives a soft-knee, dB-domain gain computer whose ratio steps from a gentle 2:1 up to a slamming NUKE all-in extreme; attack and a program-dependent release shape the punch. A DIST stage layers asymmetric 2nd/3rd-harmonic FET grit that grows as the compressor works, then a dry/wet Mix blends it back. Controls: Threshold, Ratio (gentle to NUKE), Attack, Release, Dist and Mix.

name: Choral Bank
explanation: A fully-polyphonic organ / string-ensemble synthesizer in the lineage of the big paraphonic-string machines. Every key sounds a divide-down full-keyboard source — a stack of octave and fifth partials voiced like an organ's footages — and each note gets its OWN gentle resonant low-pass with a slow attack/release swell, so dense chords each breathe independently. The whole bank pours into a rich four-tap ensemble chorus whose slow quadrature LFOs widen the sound into a vast, shimmering, angelic choir. Controls: Cutoff, Resonance, Attack, Release, Ensemble, Brightness and Level.

name: Thrift Poly
explanation: A lovable lo-fi eight-voice paraphonic synthesizer in the budget-classic tradition. Each voice is a DCO blending saw and square with a square sub an octave down, but all voices share ONE resonant low-pass filter and ONE envelope — so sweeping the Cutoff makes whole chords gurgle and breathe together, the signature paraphonic behaviour. A built-in stereo chorus adds width and a touch of wobble, while the gritty soft-clipped output keeps things charming rather than hi-fi. Controls: Cutoff, Resonance, Env Amount, Sub, Chorus, Release and Level.

name: Mono Spark
explanation: A punchy, sub-heavy monophonic synthesizer in the classic ribbon-strip lineage. One main VCO blends a saw with a variable-width pulse (PWM) and is reinforced by a strong square SUB oscillator one and two octaves down plus a touch of noise, all driven into a snappy 4-pole resonant low-pass with its own decay envelope and an amp envelope. Last-note priority mono with a 4-note hold stack for tight chord stabs — built for weighty techno/acid basslines and zippy leads.

name: Bender Fuzz
explanation: A thick, gated germanium fuzz in the vintage three-transistor lineage. Three cascaded asymmetric germanium-style clipping stages build a hard, mid-forward wall of fuzz that is grittier and more aggressive than a typical Fuzz Face or Big Muff. An envelope-tracking gate chokes the output as notes decay, giving the ragged staccato cut-off and 'spit' the circuit is famous for. A tilt Tone control shapes brightness and Level sets output. Controls: Fuzz, Gate, Tone, Level.

name: Brit Distortion
explanation: An amp-in-a-box British distortion. A tightening high-pass feeds a hot gain stage into a soft-knee/hard-rail clipper (the diode + op-amp behaviour of a cranked British amp), which then drives an interactive passive 3-band tone stack: a low shelf (Bass), a peaking bell around 650 Hz (Mid) and a high shelf (Treble). The bands load each other like a real passive network, so you can dial scooped 'crunch' or mid-forward 'roar'. Controls: Gain, Bass, Mid, Treble and Level.

name: Tube Echo
explanation: A single-head tape echo with tube-preamp warmth. One record/play tape head provides a single delayed repeat fed back on itself; a tube preamp stage adds the signature warm saturation that colours the signal even at 100% dry. Each repeat is gently darkened by tape HF loss, softly saturated, and pitch-wandered by slow wow and faster flutter. Feedback can build toward bounded self-oscillation. Controls: Time, Feedback, Drive (preamp warmth), Tone (repeat darkening) and dry/wet Mix.

name: Gush Phaser
explanation: A 4-stage OTA phaser with a gushy, watery, deep sweep. Four cascaded allpass filters are swept by a triangle LFO so the notches glide up and down the spectrum (Rate sets the speed, Depth the span). A Color control feeds part of the chain output back into its input, adding bounded resonant feedback that sharpens the sweep into a vocal, gushing whoosh. Controls: Rate, Depth, Color and dry/wet Mix.

name: Twin Filter
explanation: A dual series-filter monophonic synth: two detuned sawtooth oscillators feed a resonant HIGH-PASS filter in series with a resonant LOW-PASS filter. Because the high-pass comes first you can carve hollow, nasal, band-pass tones that single-filter monos cannot reach — the HP Cutoff thins and hollows the body, the LP Cutoff darkens the top, and where the two corners pinch together you get a reedy vocal band. Both filters are resonant and a punchy filter envelope (Env Amount + Decay) sweeps both cutoffs upward for the signature 'wow' attack. Detune thickens the two oscillators; pitch tracks the played note exactly. Up to two voices, last-note priority.

name: Vivid Poly
explanation: A bright, punchy 8-voice analog-style polyphonic synthesizer built for cutting leads and forward brass stabs. Each voice runs two DCOs — a band-limited saw and a variable-width pulse with PWM — plus a Ring control that morphs in oscillator-sync and ring-modulation grit for an aggressive metallic top end. The mix feeds a snappy resonant 4-pole low-pass driven by a fast, punchy filter envelope, then a quick amplitude AR; chords ring with independent contours and pitch tracks the keyboard. Controls: Cutoff, Resonance, Env Amount, PWM, Ring, Attack, Release and Level. Vivid and aggressive rather than mellow.

name: Matrix Poly
explanation: A deeply-modulated dual-filter analog polyphonic synthesizer with six voices. Each voice runs two oscillators (a band-limited saw and a PWM pulse) into a smooth resonant low-pass shaped by its own filter envelope. A global modulation LFO is matrix-routed at once to filter cutoff, oscillator pitch and pulse width, so held chords slowly breathe and evolve. Controls: Cutoff, Resonance, Env Amount, Mod Depth, Mod Rate, Attack, Release and Level.

name: Pocket Tone
explanation: A lo-fi mini-digital pocket synth with a charming toy-instrument character. Six voices play one of five selectable single-cycle timbres (piano, fantasy, violin, flute, guitar), each built from a short additive or pulse formula, through a simple AR amplitude contour with a touch of pitch vibrato. A BITS control adds the unmistakable cheap-digital crunch via combined bit-depth and sample-rate reduction. Controls: Voice, Attack, Release, Vibrato, Bits and Level.

name: Drip Tank
explanation: A drippy outboard spring-reverb tank — the long, boingy surf-amp spring sound, not a tight studio plate. Three parallel spring lines, each a chain of dispersive allpass filters (high frequencies arrive before low ones, giving the signature chirpy 'drip' and flutter) feeding a short damped delay loop. A built-in transient detector fires an excitation burst into the tank, so hard hits audibly boing. Controls: Mix, Dwell (send/level into the tank), Boing (excitation/drip intensity), Tone (dark to bright) and Decay (tail length). Bounded feedback — it never runs away.

name: Opto Trem
explanation: An optical stereo tremolo: a low-frequency oscillator drives a smoothed photocell-style gain cell, modelling the gentle lag of a light-dependent resistor so even hard shapes pulse musically. Shape selects the LFO contour (sine, triangle, square or ramp), Rate and Depth set the throb, and Stereo morphs from a mono tremolo to an auto-pan where the L/R cells run in anti-phase to sweep the image side to side. Clean amplitude modulation with no distortion. Controls: Rate, Depth, Shape, Stereo and Mix.

name: Sem Voice
explanation: A warm SEM-lineage duophonic synth voice. Two detuned oscillators (a saw and a variable-width pulse) feed a 12 dB/oct state-variable filter whose response morphs continuously from low-pass, through a notch, to high-pass via the Mode control. The filter has its own resonance and a decay envelope; Env Amount sets how far the cutoff sweeps, Detune thickens the two oscillators and opens the pulse, Decay shapes both the filter sweep and the amp tail, and Level sets output. The smooth low-pass -> notch -> high-pass sweep is the signature vocal, growling character.

name: Hyper VA
explanation: A modern hypersaw virtual-analog polyphonic synthesizer with eight voices. Each voice stacks seven detuned band-limited saws into a supersaw plus a square sub an octave down, runs them through a bright resonant 4-pole low-pass driven by its own envelope, then an amplitude envelope. Detune thickens the saw stack, Spread fans it across a huge stereo field, and the bright digital filter opens into shimmering trance leads and pads. Controls: Detune, Cutoff, Resonance, Env Amount, Spread, Attack, Release, Level.

name: Micro Shift
explanation: A micro-pitch detune doubler that fattens and widens a source into a lush stereo double. Two pitch-shifted voices are detuned a few cents up and down (below a semitone), each given its own short delay tap, panned hard left/right and blended with the dry signal. Fractional delay-line pitch shifting keeps it smooth with no zipper noise. Controls: Detune (cent spread), Delay (tap time), Width (stereo spread), Feedback (subtle regen) and dry/wet Mix.

name: Storm Juno
explanation: A six-voice DCO polyphonic synthesizer built for the hollow PWM 'hoover / storm' lead. Each voice stacks three detuned pulse oscillators under deep pulse-width modulation, plus a saw and a sub-octave square, into a resonant low-pass with its own punchy filter envelope, then a stereo chorus for width. The PWM Depth and Rate sweep the hollow detuned-pulse motion while Cutoff reshapes the tone — perfect for big rave-pad chords. Controls: Cutoff, Resonance, Env Amount, PWM Depth, PWM Rate, Chorus, Release and Level.

name: Cosmo Poly
explanation: A lush eight-voice analog poly synthesizer with a cinematic ring-modulated character. Each voice layers a saw and a pulse oscillator plus a sub-octave into a smooth resonant low-pass shaped by its own filter envelope, then an amplitude envelope; chords ring with independent contours across a wide stereo field. A built-in ring modulator multiplies each voice against a detuned carrier to add clangy, inharmonic metallic shimmer, while Brilliance tilts the top end open for a glassy string-pad sheen. Controls: Cutoff, Resonance, Env Amount, Ring, Brilliance, Attack, Release and Level.

name: Unison Four
explanation: A four-VCO unison mono/poly synthesizer. Every note stacks four band-limited saw/pulse oscillators that fan apart as Detune rises, piling into one fat, beating unison lead or bass — or spreading across a chord when several notes are held. Oscillator 1 hard-syncs the others (Sync) for resonant-formant grit, and the stack runs through a resonant 4-pole low-pass with its own decay envelope. Controls: Detune, Cutoff, Resonance, Env Amount, Sync, Decay and Level.

name: FM Four
explanation: A programmable 4-operator FM synth. Four sine operators are wired by a selectable algorithm — a serial stack, two parallel FM pairs, or three modulators in parallel into one carrier — for everything from clangy basses to glassy bells and evolving pads. An exposed modulator ratio and FM depth set the timbre, operator feedback adds gritty 80s-digital edge, and an attack/release envelope shapes both the amplitude and the modulation index so the brightness evolves over each note. Controls: Ratio, FM Depth, Feedback, Algorithm, Attack, Release and Level.

name: Pin Matrix
explanation: An eccentric British-modular mono/paraphonic synth instrument. Three free-running oscillators (saw, triangle, pulse) feed a ring modulator that multiplies two of them for clangy, inharmonic metallic tones, then a diode-ladder resonant low-pass that self-oscillates, all shaped by a snappy trapezoid-style envelope. Osc Mix crossfades saw against pulse over a triangle bed; Ring Mod sweeps in the bell-like inharmonic content; Cutoff and Resonance sweep and ring the filter toward self-oscillation; Env Amount and Decay drive the trapezoid's bite; pitch tracks the played note and the signal stays bounded by soft saturation.

name: West Cell
explanation: A west-coast complex-oscillator voice. A modulator sine cross-modulates (through-zero FM) a primary sine, then the result is driven into a series wavefolder that creases the wave and multiplies its harmonics, and finally a low-pass gate (a combined filter+amp) plucks the tone with a single fast control envelope for bongo/marimba/clang transients. Pitch tracks, plays chords, and stays bounded. Controls: Timbre, Fold, Gate Decay, Tone and Level.

name: Juno Glow
explanation: A warm 6-voice DCO polyphonic synthesizer. Each voice runs ONE rock-stable digitally-controlled oscillator (a band-limited saw blended with a variable-width pulse driven by PWM), a square sub-oscillator an octave down and a sip of noise, into a resonant low-pass shaped by its own envelope (Cutoff, Env Amount, Resonance) and a snappy amp contour. The whole bus then passes through the signature lush BBD-style stereo ENSEMBLE CHORUS that gives the shimmering width. Controls: Cutoff, Resonance, Env Amount, PWM, Sub, Chorus, Release and Level.

name: Oberon
explanation: A fat discrete-oscillator analog polyphonic synthesizer with six independent voices. Each voice runs two oscillators — a band-limited saw and a hollow pulse — plus a wide-detuned fat layer, into a raw resonant 2-pole (12 dB/oct) low-pass shaped by its own envelope, then an amplitude envelope. A unison Spread detunes the layers and pans each voice across the stereo field for huge, brassy late-70s American-poly stabs — deliberately fatter and rawer than a clean ladder-filter poly. Controls: Cutoff, Resonance, Env Amount, Spread, Attack, Release and Level.

name: Solo One
explanation: An aggressive monophonic lead/bass synth: two oscillators (saw + detuned pulse) hard-synced for biting grit, feeding a snappy resonant 4-pole low-pass driven by a fast, punchy filter envelope. Cutoff, Resonance and Env Amount sweep and bite the timbre; Decay sets the snap, Detune fattens, Drive adds pre-filter grit, and the voice is mono with last-note priority while pitch tracks the keyboard.

name: Air Lift
explanation: An air-band shelving EQ: the silky high-shelf specialist. A gentle, wide AIR high shelf opens and brightens the very top without harshness, with a selectable corner that climbs up into the highs (2.5k / 5k / 10k / 20k / 40k). Beneath it sit a musical LOW shelf and a broad MID bell, plus an OUTPUT trim. All bands are clean, stable biquads — boost-only air with a soft slope so the top lifts instead of spiking. Controls: Air, Air Freq, Low, Mid and Output.

name: Mu Leveler
explanation: A vari-mu tube-style compressor and leveler. It models a variable-mu valve gain stage, where the tube's effective gain falls as the signal gets louder, so compression is soft, rounded and program-dependent rather than the hard, fixed-ratio grab of a VCA or FET. Detection runs in the dB domain with a wide, soft knee; the recovery 'breathes', speeding up under dense program material and relaxing as the music opens out. A touch of warm, mostly even-harmonic valve saturation is added on the way out, growing with the Input drive. Controls: Input, Threshold, Recovery, Makeup and dry/wet Mix.

name: Vowel Box
explanation: A talk-box-style formant filter that makes your input sing vowels. Three resonant band-pass formant filters track the F1/F2/F3 peaks of the vowels A-E-I-O-U; sweep the Vowel control and the peaks morph smoothly between vowel shapes. Drive adds harmonics for the formants to bite into, Resonance sharpens the peaks, and Mix blends against the dry signal. Best on rich, harmonic sources.

name: Jove Eight
explanation: A lush eight-voice analog-style polyphonic synthesizer in the flagship-poly lineage. Each voice stacks TWO oscillators (a band-limited saw and a variable-width pulse with slow PWM) plus a sub-oscillator one octave down, spread by a subtle unison detune for the big stacked-string-pad character. The mixed oscillators feed a resonant 24 dB/oct low-pass driven by its own envelope (Cutoff, Env Amount, Resonance), then an amplitude envelope. Chords ring with independent contours. Controls: Cutoff, Resonance, Env Amount, PW, Detune, Attack, Release and Level.

name: Bus Glue
explanation: A VCA-style stereo bus compressor for the classic mix-glue character. A feed-forward, RMS-ish level detector watches the summed program and drives a smooth soft-knee gain reduction computed in the dB domain. The same gain is applied to both channels (stereo-linked) so the image stays put while the mix tightens and punches. Three switchable ratios (2:1 / 4:1 / 10:1) plus Attack and Release shape the gain-reduction envelope; Makeup restores level and Mix blends in the dry signal for parallel glue. Loud, dense material is pulled together more than quiet passages, gluing a mix into a cohesive whole. Controls: Threshold, Ratio, Attack, Release, Makeup and Mix.

name: Lattice Verb
explanation: A classic algorithmic reverb built from the textbook Schroeder/Moorer topology: four parallel feedback comb filters with prime delay lengths are summed, each with a one-pole high-frequency damper in its feedback path, then passed through two series allpass diffusers that smear the echoes into a smooth, controllable tail. Controls: Mix, Size (scales the comb delay lengths), Decay (comb feedback / RT60), Damping (HF loss per pass) and Pre-Delay.

name: Stereo Room
explanation: An early-digital stereo room reverb. A panned multitap network throws out tight, defined early reflections off the room walls, feeding a Schroeder allpass diffuser into a short-to-medium feedback delay network for a controlled diffuse tail. Gentle damping plus subtle feedback quantisation give the slightly grainy vintage-digital character. Controls: Mix, Size, Decay, Diffusion and Pre-Delay. Mix at 0 is the dry signal.

name: Diode Comp
explanation: A diode-bridge style bus compressor with a smooth, slightly coloured character. A stereo-linked peak/RMS detector feeds a dB-domain gain computer with a wide over-easy soft knee; Attack and Release shape the gain-reduction envelope. The signal passes through a gentle asymmetric diode-bridge transfer that adds a touch of even-harmonic warmth, so loud material is squeezed and coloured more than quiet passages for a classy, glued sound. Controls: Threshold, Ratio, Attack, Release and Makeup.

name: Additive
explanation: A polyphonic additive (Fourier) synthesizer with eight independent voices. Each voice sums up to 32 sine harmonics whose amplitudes follow a sculptable spectral shape: an Odd/Even partial balance (square-hollow vs. full saw-like timbres), a Brightness spectral tilt, and a Spectral Decay that lets higher harmonics fade faster than the fundamental over the life of the note. An amplitude Attack/Release contour gates each voice and chords ring with independent decays. Controls: Odd, Even, Brightness, Spectral Decay, Attack, Release and Level.

name: Reed Piano
explanation: A polyphonic electric reed-piano instrument modelled on the warm 60s struck-steel-reed school pianos. Each voice is a hollow reed-tone oscillator run through an asymmetric soft clipper whose drive tracks velocity and a fast 'bark' envelope, so hard hits growl and bark on the attack then mellow into a hollow sustain — distinct from a tine Rhodes. A snappy amp envelope with adjustable body decay, a one-pole tone tilt and a built-in sine tremolo finish the voice. Up to 12 voices, so chords ring out. Controls: Bark, Decay, Tone, Tremolo Depth, Tremolo Rate and Level.

name: Combo Organ
explanation: A polyphonic 1960s transistor combo organ. A divide-down design: every key fires one master pitch that is divided into octaves and re-summed through three bright rocker tabs — 16' Bass, 8' Mid and a 4'+reed Bright — for the hollow, buzzy, slightly reedy combo tone. Keys speak instantly (fast attack/release, no slow envelope), a scanner-style Vibrato wobbles the whole keyboard, and a Tone tilt runs the sound from dark and woody to bright and cutting. Controls: Bass (16'), Mid (8'), Bright (4'+reed), Vibrato Depth, Vibrato Rate, Tone and Level. Cheerful, bright, and fully polyphonic so chords ring.

name: Clav
explanation: An 8-voice plucked-string funk-clavier instrument. Each voice is an independent Karplus-Strong string: a short velocity-scaled excitation burst is injected into a tuned, fractional delay line whose feedback loop carries a damping low-pass, so the string snaps then rings down naturally. A magnetic-style comb pickup tap and a brightness tilt colour the percussive, springy tone; chords play with independent decays. Controls: Attack, Decay, Brightness, Pickup, Damping and Level.

name: Tine Piano
explanation: A polyphonic electric tine-piano instrument. Each voice is a struck metal tine modelled with two-operator FM — a sine carrier at pitch plus a higher-ratio modulator whose index decays fast for the bright bell/bark attack that melts into a mellow sine body. Velocity drives the bark amount and brightness, a natural two-stage decay keeps notes singing, and a gentle opposed-phase stereo tremolo adds the classic suitcase sway. Controls: Bell (attack FM amount), Decay, Tone, Tremolo Depth, Tremolo Rate and Level.

name: Tonewheel Organ
explanation: A polyphonic additive drawbar organ. Every held key is built from a stack of sine partials tuned to the classic footages (16', 8', 5 1/3', 4', 2 2/3', 2', 1') that you blend with the drawbars, so chords ring as a full console organ. Instant attack and flat sustain while held — no decay — plus a snappy key-click, a single-trigger percussion tap (selectable 2nd or 3rd harmonic), tube drive and a built-in rotary speaker that adds tremolo and a doppler shimmer. Controls: Sub 16', Fund 8', Third 5 1/3', Upper brightness, Percussion, Perc Mode, Drive and Rotary speed.

name: Star Field
explanation: A multitap space reverb. A network of discrete, scattered early-reflection taps pings outward across the stereo field and then blossoms into a diffuse, gently modulated ambient tail — a constellation of reflections decaying into deep space rather than a smooth hall. Controls: Size, Taps/Spread, Decay, Modulation and dry/wet Mix.

name: Iron Drive
explanation: Transformer-style iron-core saturation. The iron saturates earliest in the lows, so a tunable low-end push is fed back into the drive stage, a hysteresis-flavoured soft saturator adds asymmetric even + odd harmonic warmth, and a gentle high-frequency rounding tames the top. A post Tone tilt, dry/wet Mix and Output trim finish it. Controls: Drive, Low, Tone, Mix and Output.

name: Vox Strings
explanation: A paraphonic string + voice ensemble. Every held note stacks three slightly detuned sawtooth strings against a breathy, formant-filtered choir 'aah' layer; the two blend, swell through a slow attack/release contour, and pour into a lush three-phase ensemble chorus that spreads the section across the stereo field. Controls: Strings Level, Voice Level, Ensemble, Attack, Release, Tone and Level.

name: Vector Wave
explanation: A polyphonic vector / wave-sequencing synthesizer. Four single-cycle waves are generated in code and placed on the corners of a square morph pad; a Vector X/Y joystick bilinearly crossfades between them while a slow Wave-Sequence rate walks that point around the pad so the timbre evolves over time on a held note. Up to eight voices keyed per note, each running two slightly detuned vector oscillators into a resonant low-pass with an attack/release contour. Controls: Vector X, Vector Y, Sequence Rate, Cutoff, Attack, Release, Detune and Level.

name: Discrete EQ
explanation: A punchy, forward discrete-electronics console equaliser. Three cascaded bands modelled on a vintage 500-series discrete-op-amp EQ: a low band that switches between musical shelf and bell, a sweepable mid bell with reciprocal proportional-Q (the bell sits wide at small boosts and narrows as you push it hard), and a high band that switches between shelf and bell. The whole strip is followed by a touch of discrete-stage drive for that forward console weight. Controls: Low Gain, Low Shape (shelf/bell), Mid Freq, Mid Gain, High Gain, High Shape (shelf/bell) and Drive.

name: Multiband
explanation: A three-band dynamics processor for mastering. The signal is split into low, mid and high bands by two crossovers (~220 Hz and ~2.5 kHz), and each band is compressed independently with its own threshold so the lows can pump without dulling the highs. A shared ratio sets how hard each band squeezes; the bands recombine through a makeup/output trim. Controls: Low Thresh, Mid Thresh, High Thresh, Ratio and Output.

name: Patch 2600
explanation: A monophonic semi-modular synthesizer in the spirit of the classic patchable bench instruments. Three oscillators (saw, pulse and triangle) are spread by a shared Detune and can be cross-multiplied by a switchable ring modulator for metallic clangor, then driven into an aggressive resonant low-pass. A dedicated filter ADSR (scaled by Filter Env Amount) sweeps the cutoff while a separate amplitude envelope shapes the level, with Glide for portamento and last-note priority. Controls: Detune, RingMod, Cutoff, Resonance, FilterEnvAmt, Attack, Release, Glide and Level.

name: Wave Table
explanation: A polyphonic wavetable synthesizer in the spirit of early-1980s German digital hardware. Eight single-cycle waves are generated in code, spanning a pure sine through ever richer harmonic spectra into bright, hollow formant shapes. A Wave Position control scans smoothly through the bank with linear interpolation between tables, morphing the timbre in real time. Each of up to eight voices runs a wavetable oscillator into a resonant 4-pole low-pass shaped by its own filter envelope and amount, then an amplitude envelope, so chords ring with independent contours. Controls: Wave Position, Cutoff, Resonance, Filter Env Amount, Attack, Release, Detune and Level.

name: Studio Delay
explanation: A pristine rackmount digital delay. A fractionally-interpolated stereo delay line gives crystal-clear echoes that decay smoothly; feedback regenerates the repeats (bounded near maximum so it never runs away), an optional gentle modulation adds subtle movement, a high/low damping filter darkens or brightens each repeat, and a stereo width control offsets the left/right taps for a wide image. Controls: Time, Feedback, Modulation, Damping, Width and dry/wet Mix.

name: Phase Synth
explanation: A polyphonic phase-distortion digital synthesizer. Instead of analog oscillators and filters, each of its up to eight voices reads a sine table through a warped phase ramp: a non-linear phase map bends the read so a pure sine morphs toward saw, square and resonant timbres. The warp depth is driven both by a static Shape control and by a per-voice distortion (DCW) envelope, so notes ring bright on the attack and settle toward a sine as they decay. Controls: Shape (sine to bright), DCW Amount, DCW Decay, Attack, Release, Detune and Level.

name: Sonic Max
explanation: A psychoacoustic clarity and phase enhancer. The signal is split into low, mid and high bands; the lows are delayed a touch to re-align their timing against the highs, restoring transient punch. Process lifts high-frequency presence for air and definition, while Lo Contour blooms the low end for weight and body. The bands recombine through a soft output trim and dry/wet Mix, so the effect stays subtle and bounded. Controls: Process, Lo Contour, Output and Mix.

name: Robot Voice
explanation: A channel vocoder in the classic analog-bank style. Your input is the modulator: it is split into 8, 12 or 16 band-pass bands, each tracked by an envelope follower. An internal carrier — a buzzy sawtooth blended toward white noise — is filtered into the same bands and multiplied by those envelopes, so the carrier is forced to wear the spectral shape of your input. The result is the unmistakable talking, robotic timbre. Controls: Bands, Carrier (saw to noise), Formant band shift, Resonance and dry/wet Mix.

name: Duo Synth
explanation: A duophonic analog lead synthesizer. Two sawtooth oscillators independently track the two most recent held notes (lowest note -> OSC 1, newest -> OSC 2), so two keys sound as two distinct pitches; a single key locks both oscillators together. An optional hard-sync resets OSC 2 to OSC 1 for a buzzy, formant-rich tone, and the pair feeds a shared resonant 4-pole low-pass swept by one ADSR for a sharp, punchy character. Controls: Detune, Sync, Cutoff, Resonance, Env Amount, Attack, Release and Level.

name: Cinematic Poly
explanation: A lush, grand polyphonic synthesizer for cinematic brass-leaning pads and swells. Up to eight voices keyed by note, each running two detuned layers of saw + pulse oscillators into their own resonant low-pass with a filter ADSR, then an amplitude ADSR; a gentle global ensemble chorus and brightness tilt add the wide, choral sheen. Controls: Detune, Cutoff, Resonance, Filter Env Amount, Attack, Release, Brightness and Level.

name: Whammy
explanation: An extreme expression-pedal pitch shifter. A dual-pointer, crossfaded delay-line shifter chases a target pitch ratio that the Pedal sweeps from dry (heel) to full shift (toe), so you can dive-bomb and soar in real time. Mode selects the direction — 0 up, 1 down, 2 a shimmering dual-octave stack — across a wide ±24-semitone Pitch range. Crossfaded read pointers keep big pitch leaps and dives click-free. Controls: Pitch, Pedal, Mode and dry/wet Mix.

name: Squelch Filter
